Anxiety lives in your chest. Breathing reaches it faster than anything else I have tried in 65 years of practice.

Comment BREATHE and I will send you my 2-minute breathing guide.

Save this for the next time your chest feels tight.

#anxie

Do you know what your core values are?

Not what your parents said. Not what your school said. Not what the government says. Yours.

Take 10 or 15 minutes and write them down. What are you willing to live with? What's a deal-breaker? What k

A patient of mine failed the bar exam to become a lawyer. Nine times.

He passed on the tenth.

Do you know what they call him now? Lawyer.

Every time you fail, you learn something. Every miss teaches you something a success can't. That's

Your mood is not something that happens to you.

It's something you create. And recreate. Over and over throughout the day.

Watch a baby — happy, then crying, then laughing, all in two minutes. Adults aren't that different. The difference

The past walks into your head when you are not looking for it. Three in the morning. Driving home. Mid-conversation.

When it shows up, most people try to rewrite it. It does not work. You cannot remake the past.

You can review the past. Y

How I stay optimistic at age 85

Comment health below, and I’ll send you my free pre-85 Happiness Playbook—a guide packed with tools to improve your well-being.

Who is in control of your mood? There is only one person. You.

If you do not like the mood you are in, change it. Yes, you can. Mood is yours to change.

I made a short guide and workbook detailing the exact practice. Comment MOOD and I'll

40 percent of the country is depressed right now.

And we have a cure for most of it. It's exercise. The problem is — when you're depressed, you don't feel like moving. That's the trap.

Here's how you break it: 5 minutes. Today, or tomorro

The political arguments, the paycheck-to-paycheck headlines, the people calling each other names — that machine is not shutting off.

But your inner mellow? That's yours. And it is not dependen
Breathing the number one tool for emotional control.

Most people worry about either what's gonna happen in the future or what has happened in the past. The antidote — stay in the present. In the present, nothing bad is most likely happening to you. Some people are having bad things happen, bu

People relate to what they hear in their head as if that's who they are.

But your brain is a tool. The same way your hand is a tool. Your hand doesn't tell you where to go. You tell your hand where to go.

The exact same thing is true for
65 years treating anxiety. The cure? Breathe it off or burn it off. No pills needed.

Most people make this mistake at the doctor.

They go in alone.

When something is wrong, you’re usually anxious or scared.
That’s not the best time to take in important information.

So bring someone with you.

A friend. An advocate.

They

If you don’t feel good, start here.

Find one thing you’re grateful for.

Just one.

Most people try to fix what’s going on inside… from the outside.

Food.
Alcohol.
Drugs.
Gambling.
Distractions.

They’re all attempts to change how we feel internally.

But it doesn’t work for long.

Because the problem isn’t out there.

It

You’re criticizing yourself thousands of times a year.

You’re talking to yourself all day long.

And much of it is negative.

“That’s just my luck.”
“There I go again.”
“I should’ve done better.”

They seem small.

But five or ten of these

The mind records everything.

Everything you say to yourself gets stored.

If you criticize yourself 10 times a day, that’s 3,600 times a year.

Think about that.

3,600 negative messages going into your mind and settling into your body.

A

If you talk to yourself negatively, the mind remembers every single thing that you said.

What happens then, if you say 10 things a day that are negative in some way. This always happens to me. I'm the kind of person, I can't do that. That

At eighty-seven, I’ve come to trust something simple: Change does not come from intensity.

It comes from repetition.

It grows through action.

You don’t need:

You need a beginning.

I started with what I could do.
I repeated it.
And my body responded.

This week, I wanted to offer something simple and practical.

Anxiety and intrusive thoughts are two of the most common struggles I’ve seen in my clinical work over the past six decades. They don’t usually arrive all at once.

Anxiety is not a mystery.
It’s restless energy in the body that hasn’t found a way out.
